Text:
Not at the QBR. Not in the save call when the health score flips yellow. Those are theatre.

The decision happens quietly in the first 90 days, when the customer's brain settles on whether they will still be paying you in two years.

Here is the part nobody wants to hear.

Onboarding is the only phase of the lifecycle where CS holds unilateral control. Pricing belongs to the CRO. Expansion drifted to the AE. The roadmap belongs to Product. The customer's politics belong to the customer.

But the depth of the deployment? The number of teams that depend on you? Whether a real outcome gets co-signed by the budget holder? Those are yours. No permission needed.

And what do we do with the only lever we fully own?

We optimise it for speed. We hand the account off as fast as the implementation plan allows. We hit the go-live target and move on.

That is the most expensive mistake in customer success. It is invisible because the damage does not surface for eleven months.

I broke down why onboarding is your only fully controlled renewal, and the metric that actually predicts it, here: https://churnisdead.com/newsletter/onboarding-only-renewal-you-control

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #Retention

Every onboarding metric we love optimises for the vendor's dashboard.

Time to first value. Time to go-live. Time to activation.

What do they all measure? The day the account moves from implementing to live in your CRM and stops consuming your implementation hours.

None of them measure whether the customer built a dependency they cannot easily unwind.

So let me name the number that actually matters. Depth of root.

Did the product get embedded into a workflow the customer cannot rip out? Or did you just deliver a login?

A login is fast. A root is slow.

And every day you shave off implementation to hit a go-live target, you are borrowing against a renewal you will lose in month eleven. When a new VP arrives. When budget review lands. When the one person who understood why they bought you takes another job.

The login churns quietly. The root survives all three.

We measure speed because speed is easy to measure. Depth is hard. So we optimise for the thing that photographs well and lose the thing that keeps the account.

What does your onboarding actually optimise for, honestly? Speed or root?

#CustomerSuccess #Onboarding #ChurnReduction

The accounts that hurt most are not the dramatic churns. They are the quiet ones. Fine accounts. Went live early. Someone was proud of them.

They left because you won the wrong game in the first 90 days.

Five signs you are shipping a login, not growing a root:

1. Success is defined as go-live, not a business outcome co-signed by the budget holder.

2. One team uses the product. You never expanded the footprint during onboarding when you had the momentum.

3. Your only relationship is the champion. When they leave, so does the account.

4. Nobody inside the customer would struggle to remove you. Ripping you out is a Tuesday task, not a project.

5. You measured speed. Time to value, time to activation. Never depth.

Here is the uncomfortable math. Every day shaved off implementation to hit a go-live target is a day borrowed against a renewal you lose in month eleven.

The renewal was never yours to win at renewal. It was yours in month one. Most of us hand it back.

Full breakdown plus the Dependency Window Audit in the link. Link in comments.

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #CustomerRetention

Spend a decade in this function and you learn something uncomfortable.

The dramatic churns do not haunt you. The product failures. The pricing fights. The accounts that blew up. You saw those coming. You could explain them.

The ones that hurt are the quiet ones.

The accounts that were fine. That went live early. That someone on the team was genuinely proud of. The ones you moved on from because they looked done.

They did not leave because I failed at the renewal.

They left because I won the wrong game in the first 90 days. I delivered a login when I could have grown a root. Then I chased the next go-live before the account had anything holding it in place.

That took me years to see. Because the damage never shows up when you make the mistake. It surfaces eleven months later, when a new VP arrives or the champion takes another job, and by then the story you tell yourself is that it was out of your hands.

It was in your hands. In month one. You just handed it back.

I write about the parts of CS nobody puts on the dashboard. Subscribe here: https://churnisdead.com
